bull_thesis: Amadeus is the entrenched B2B technology backbone of global travel, trading at roughly 10x price-to-free-cash-flow (from our scan) and about 13-14x trailing-twelve-month (TTM, the last four reported quarters) enterprise value to free cash flow (EV/FCF, the company's total value including debt divided by its free cash flow). It earns a 32% free-cash-flow margin, beats its own earnings guidance 86% of the time, and has compounded earnings per share at a 27.6% annual rate over three years and free cash flow at 33.8%. Three durable legs, each protected by high switching costs (the cost and disruption a customer faces to move to a rival), carry the story: (1) Air IT Solutions (fiscal-year 2025 revenue EUR2,346M, contribution EUR1,664M, about a 71% contribution margin) sells multi-year, mission-critical passenger-system and retailing migrations (Altea, now Nevio/Stratos) that are extraordinarily hard to leave; (2) Air Distribution (FY25 revenue EUR3,119M, contribution EUR1,563M) is the global distribution system and travel-platform network connecting airlines and travel sellers, with revenue-per-booking up 5% and NDC (a newer airline-direct content standard) aggregation widening the market it can serve; (3) Hospitality & Other (FY25 revenue EUR1,052M, contribution EUR379M, up 10.9%) is winning new central-reservation-system business against Marriott, Accor, MGM and Ascott as those go live. The worry bears pressed hardest in 2025 (the pace at which customers convert to Nevio/Stratos, pushed by analysts James Goodall and Sven Merkt) has resolved in Amadeus's favor in 2026: TUI signed on as the launch customer for Navitaire Stratos and Lufthansa Group adopted Nevio, validating the re-platforming thesis. The balance sheet is deleveraged at roughly 1.0x net-debt-to-EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization), with a Piotroski score of 7 out of 9 and an Altman-Z of 3.0 (both health checks pointing to a solid, solvent business), plus a EUR500M share buyback running March to June 2026 that mops up legacy share dilution. For this to hold: air traffic needs to stabilize (the FY26 guidance assumes about 3% traffic growth and a second-half recovery, both maintained); the shift to AI and agentic (AI that acts on a traveler's behalf) booking needs to be captured by Amadeus's NDC/Nevio/data-mesh stack rather than routing around it; and the recent gross-margin squeeze needs to prove to be temporary reinvestment (the cloud migration finishing in early 2026, with research and development around 20% of sales defending the franchise). One clear peer lever: roughly 10x price-to-free-cash-flow against Booking Holdings at about 15x, on comparable net margins near 20%, is a genuine discount for a B2B infrastructure business versus consumer-facing online travel agencies.
bear_thesis: The apparent cheapness sits on a franchise whose core toll-booth is under structural challenge, and whose reported growth slowed sharply in 2025 (revenue up 6.1% year over year versus a 13.3% three-year annual growth rate), dressed in constant-currency and currency-adjusted language to keep an 'acceleration' story alive that the actual reported figures do not support. Three real cracks: (1) the gross margin is contracting (27.8%, about 0.57 standard deviations below its historical peak) while research and development runs near 20% of revenue and the cloud migration weighs on costs, so the company is investing heavily just to stand still and the 'margin expansion' is an operating-profit illusion sitting on an eroding gross line; (2) the risk that AI and agentic booking cuts out the middleman is now an openly acknowledged industry debate, not a fringe fear: management itself concedes that airlines selling direct 'is clearly a reality,' and global-distribution-system chief executives (Amadeus included) have publicly said AI is worsening look-to-book ratios (how many searches it takes to produce one booking), which threatens the per-search economics of the Air Distribution toll-booth; (3) the pricing tailwind is maturing, with management admitting the contract resets (from full-content to free-negotiation terms) that drove the 5% lift in revenue-per-booking 'probably will not be there.' The 'catalyst' is thin for a special situation: the EUR500M buyback is only about 1.6% of market value and roughly 3.5% of shares, it is routine, it merely offsets dilution, and it is already largely executed (so any re-rating it could fund is mostly spent). The company's capital-allocation signals point to bolt-on acquisitions as the preferred use of cash, not faster shareholder returns. Return on invested capital near 9.9% is solid but not exceptional for the multiple the bull case implies. What would confirm the bear view: a quarter where Air Distribution revenue-per-booking turns negative, or a tech-native or agentic player capturing airline retailing at scale, either of which would push the global-distribution-system leg toward a terminal-decline valuation.
consensus_delta: Framed for a deep-value read: is the market pricing terminal decline into a company whose earnings power is actually stable to growing? Partly. The bearish market view assumes the global-distribution-system toll-booth is a melting asset under AI and agentic disintermediation and worsening look-to-book ratios (searches per booking). The stock backs that up: it sits 20% up its 52-week range, 11% below its 200-day moving average, with a smart-money score of 7 out of 100, meaning positioning is washed out and fearful. What the market under-weights: (a) 57% of group contribution comes from Air IT plus Hospitality (high-switching-cost software, not the contested distribution leg), and those legs are growing (Air IT contribution up, Hospitality up 10.9%); (b) the worry bears pressed hardest (the pace of Nevio/Stratos conversion) resolved favorably in 2026 (TUI on Stratos, Lufthansa on Nevio), so the market was on the wrong side of the re-platforming story; (c) the 86% earnings-beat rate, 27.6% three-year earnings-per-share growth, and 32% free-cash-flow margin are inconsistent with a terminal-decline business. What the market has right (so this is not a clean disagreement): 2025 genuinely slowed (revenue up 6.1% versus the 13.3% three-year rate), gross margin is contracting, and the agentic-distribution risk is real, not imagined. So the gap between our view and the market's is modest, not extreme: the market is fairly pricing a good business with a real but manageable secular overhang, and the consensus 12-month price target of EUR67.55 already implies about 30% upside, meaning the market is not maximally bearish on price even as positioning is light.
forcing_function: For a deep-value re-rating, the trigger is recognition that earnings power is stable to growing, not the buyback. The dated items: (1) the EUR500M buyback completing June 29, 2026 (mechanical, but modest and already largely spent on the re-rating); (2) the February 2026 capital-allocation update that Borg explicitly pre-committed to ('we'll come back in February with any changes'), the real catalyst for a step-change in shareholder returns or a new program, which is now partly behind us (the FY25 results and the EUR500M program landed in late February 2026); (3) the next Nevio/Stratos network-carrier win, the single yes-or-no event that would break the 'conversion has stalled' bear case, with the pipeline described as 'high engagement' as of the third quarter of 2025 and TUI/Lufthansa already de-risking it, so each new full-service-carrier signing is a one-sided re-rating trigger; (4) days to next earnings around 40 (the second-quarter / first-half 2026 report), which reads on the promised second-half traffic recovery and on whether gross margin turns up as the cloud migration finishes. None of these is a hard, dated, binding special-situation trigger; they are operational milestones, which is why this is a watchlist name rather than an aggressive entry.
valley_of_death: Near-term risks (the next 3 to 9 months) before any re-rating: (1) soft air traffic, with management flagging 'progressive deterioration' through 2025 (April worse than May worse than June) and FY26 guidance assuming only about 3% traffic with a second-half-weighted recovery, so a weak summer or second-half report (Middle East and geopolitical risk, the live first-quarter 2026 'Middle East travel disruptions') would pressure bookings and per-booking pricing and feed the decline narrative; (2) free-cash-flow seasonality, with Amadeus repeatedly guiding free cash flow down year over year in the first half on capital-spending, working-capital and tax timing, so a soft cash-flow quarter is a recurring optics trap that punishes the stock ahead of the recovery; (3) gross-margin optics, since until the cloud migration fully completes (early 2026) and research-and-development intensity eases, gross margin stays squeezed and gives bears a quarterly talking point; (4) catalyst exhaustion, because the EUR500M buyback ends June 29, 2026, and once it stops the mechanical buying support disappears and capital-allocation attention shifts to lower-conviction bolt-on acquisitions, removing near-term support. There is no debt-maturity wall or cash-burn cliff (1.0x leverage, 32% free-cash-flow margin); the risky window here is about narrative and the cycle, not solvency.
positioning_washout: The shareholder base is quality-growth and index money, not yield-focused, so there is no dividend-cut or passive-mandate forced selling to take advantage of. Positioning is already washed out on the long side: a smart-money score of 7 out of 100, institutional flow at 50 out of 100 (neutral), the price 20% up its 52-week range and 11% below its 200-day moving average, and an insider buy ratio of 0.00. The flow that does matter mechanically is the EUR500M buyback (its second EUR150M tranche launched June 5, running to June 29, 2026), which provides a price-insensitive bid, but it is small (about 1.6% of market value) and ends this month, so the washout is a sentiment low without a forced seller to lean against. No capitulation flush is structurally forced here; the de-rating has been gradual, not a liquidation.
moderator_conclusion: The call is to wait for the wash-out, conviction 3, with a value conviction of 4, and the two diverge on purpose. Applying the regime (CATALYST_WATCH_REGIME.md, 2026-06-05): the regime rewards hard-dated, structural special situations in thinly covered, distressed or forced-seller corners, and penalizes theses that hinge on a single shared macro factor or on soft, recurring catalysts. Amadeus fails the regime's catalyst test on both counts: its only 'event' is a small, recurring, dilution-offsetting EUR500M buyback that ends June 29 (a soft, non-binding catalyst with no contractual payout, already spent on the re-rating), and its core risk is exactly the single dominant secular factor the regime is most wary of, namely AI and agentic distribution (a risk shared across the whole global-distribution-system sector, not company-specific edge). That caps the catalyst-aware conviction at 3: this is a fine business, but there is no one-sided, dated trigger to justify an aggressive entry, and the base-case sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) of about EUR54 is only roughly 4% above the spot price of EUR52.1. The regime tilt therefore correctly deprioritizes it next to a hard special situation. Separately, the value case (value conviction 4, setting catalyst and regime aside) is stronger: a wide-moat, 32%-free-cash-flow-margin, 86%-beat compounder with a clean delivery record, trading at about 10x price-to-free-cash-flow (from our scan) below a discounted-cash-flow value of EUR59.3 and a consensus target of EUR67.55, with 57% of contribution in genuinely sticky software legs and the key bear worry (Nevio/Stratos) already resolving. It is not capped at 3 because the moat is wide and durable and the secular threat is manageable rather than terminal: Amadeus is the re-platformer of the distribution shift, not its casualty. If this were a high-but-falling-returns melting global-distribution system, the value cap would bite, but the returns and cash conversion are holding. Timing: wait for a capitulation flush, a weak second-half 2026 traffic or cash-flow report, or a fresh Middle East or oil shock (a live regime tripwire), that drags the stock toward its 52-week low and the bear-case sum-of-the-parts of EUR45.7, where the risk and reward toward the EUR62-67 bull case becomes compelling and the wide moat can be bought at a genuine discount. The specific event to wait for is the second-quarter / first-half 2026 earnings (about 40 days out) confirming the gross-margin turn (cloud migration complete) and the second-half traffic recovery, ideally into price weakness. Do not chase at EUR52, only about 4% below base-case fair value; accumulate on the flush.
sop_breakdown: Base-case segment sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately, using reconciled mid-range multiples on FY25 contribution-allocated EBITDA, with EUR2,320M group EBITDA, about 450M shares, and net debt of EUR2.68B subtracted): Air IT Solutions roughly EUR1,070M EBITDA at 13.5x equals EUR14.4B (the durable, high-switching-cost core); Air Distribution roughly EUR1,005M at 9.5x equals EUR9.55B (the global-distribution-system toll-booth, honestly marked down for the agentic and look-to-book overhang); Hospitality & Other roughly EUR244M at 12x equals EUR2.93B. That gives an enterprise value of about EUR26.9B; less net debt of EUR2.68B leaves equity of about EUR24.2B, or roughly EUR54 per share. It triangulates against TTM (trailing-twelve-month) free cash flow of EUR1.74B at a peer-marked-down 15x EV/FCF (about EUR48-50 of equity per share) on the low end, and a discounted-cash-flow value of EUR59.3 on the high end; a Buffett-style value of EUR73.3 and the consensus target of EUR67.55 sit above and reflect a less-marked-down distribution leg. The base of EUR54 is deliberately set below the discounted-cash-flow and consensus figures to credit the secular overhang on Air Distribution.
risk_reward: At about EUR52.1 (live): downside to the bear breakpoint of roughly EUR45.7 is about -12%; upside to base-case fair value of about EUR54 is about +4%; upside to the bull case and consensus of EUR62-67.5 is +19% to +30%. The asymmetry is modestly favorable on the bull tail, but base-case fair value is only about 4% above the current price: this is a quality compounder trading near a reconciled-fair sum-of-the-parts (each business line valued separately), not a deep mispricing. Risk and reward run roughly 1.5-2.5 to 1 toward the bull case and about 1 to 1 to the base case; the edge is in owning a wide-moat compounder at a fair-to-slightly-cheap price, not in a catalyst-driven re-rating.
peer_comps_note: The peer file's verdict is 'cheap versus peers' (cheap against online-travel-agency peers Booking Holdings at about 15x, Expedia, and Trip.com on price-to-free-cash-flow; fairly valued against its own global-distribution-system infrastructure moat). The right comparison set is travel-technology infrastructure, not generic consumer cyclicals: roughly 10x price-to-free-cash-flow (from our scan) and about 13-14x TTM (trailing-twelve-month) EV/FCF (enterprise value to free cash flow) against Booking Holdings at about 15x, on comparable net margins near 20%, is a genuine B2B-infrastructure discount to consumer online-travel cyclicality. But the fair read is moderate: Booking Holdings' premium is partly earned (higher growth and returns), and Amadeus's Air Distribution leg faces the same secular look-to-book and agentic-distribution overhang that pressures global-distribution-system multiples across the sector (Sabre included), so that portion of the discount is a shared sector factor, not company-specific edge, and I do not credit the full gap as name-specific advantage. The clean advantage is the Air IT and Hospitality switching-cost legs (about 57% of contribution), where the software-like stickiness justifies a premium the blended multiple under-credits. No peer override or anchor multiple was supplied; the peer multiples used (Booking Holdings at about 15x) are directional.
interrogator_dossier:
# A deep dive on management and the setup — Amadeus IT Group, S.A. (AMS.MC)

*Reviewer: Claude Opus 4.8, Investment Committee. Transcripts span the FY2024 results call (2025-02-28) through the third-quarter 2025 call (2025-11-07), cross-referenced against the screener's financial trajectory and live 2026 facts.*

---

## 1. Narrative arc

Across four straight calls the core story is strikingly stable in substance and steadily shifting in tone. The spine never changes: Amadeus is the B2B technology backbone of global travel, spanning Air Distribution (the global distribution system and travel platform), Airline IT (the Altea/Navitaire passenger systems, now Nevio retailing), and Hospitality & Other Solutions (central-reservation systems, payments, media). What evolves is which leg carries the narrative weight and how defensively management frames the macro backdrop.

- **FY2024 call (2025-02-28), peak offense.** Maroto opens on 'double digit growth and expanding profitability,' 13% revenue, 13% EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization), free cash flow of EUR1.3B (up 16%), leverage at 0.9x. The two main growth stories are (a) NDC aggregation, the newer airline-direct content standard ('we aim to become the undisputed aggregator of NDC content,' with 70-plus agreements), and (b) Nevio, the next-generation airline-retailing platform, with Air France-KLM joining British Airways, Saudia and Finnair as a fourth signed customer. Hospitality is framed as a land-grab (Marriott, Accor, MGM implementations). The capital-allocation headline is a fresh EUR1.3B buyback to clean up the 2020 convertible and equity-raise dilution.
- **Q1-2025 (2025-05-08), first defensive notes.** New chief financial officer Carol Borg debuts. Group revenue up 9%, but the tone turns cautious: 'Despite the broad uncertainty in the environment,' bookings growth slowing (travel-platform bookings up 3% versus up 9% in the fourth quarter of 2024), and management pivots to reporting excluding foreign-exchange effects because of 'high USD-euro exchange rate volatility beyond what we could foresee.' Free cash flow is down 22% year over year on capital-spending, working-capital and tax timing, the first time the cash-flow story needs defending rather than celebrating.
- **H1-2025 (2025-08-01), narrative simplification and macro management.** Management explicitly 'made some changes to our quarterly presentation... to simplify how we communicate.' Revenue up 8% in constant currency. The story leans harder on cloud transformation (90% of applications in the public cloud) and on AI and agentic AI (AI that acts on a user's behalf) as the forward narrative, and on Google and Microsoft partnerships. A telling admission: traffic showed 'progressive deterioration' through the half (April better than May better than June), with the bullish pivot being 'hopefully, we have seen the bottom.'
- **Q3-2025 (2025-11-07), re-acceleration claim and AI offense.** 'Strong third quarter full of momentum, which drove revenue growth acceleration and margin expansion.' New customer wins (Ascott in hospitality; Qatar, Vietnam, Jazeera, Singapore Airlines in revenue management). The forward narrative is now overwhelmingly AI and agentic AI ('over 500 potential use cases,' 'first data mesh in travel'), a deliberate re-framing of the company as an AI-leverage story rather than a cyclical-bookings story.

**Quietly dropped or softened:** the bookings-volume growth story faded from headline to footnote as 2025 air traffic normalized; by the third quarter the emphasis moved to revenue-per-unit (price per booking up 5%, revenue per passenger boarded up 5%) and structural growth in IT and hospitality. **Newly introduced:** AI and agentic AI as the central forward thesis, and Navitaire Stratos (the low-cost-carrier sibling to Nevio).

---

## 2. Claims versus financials (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | Financial evidence | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| 'Double digit growth... free cash flow EUR1.3B (+16%)' | FY24 | Revenue 6.14B (2024) to 6.52B (2025); net income 1.25B to 1.34B; 3-year revenue annual growth +13.3%; free-cash-flow 3-year annual growth +33.8% | **Corroborated.** The growth was real and cash-backed. |
| 'Profit grew faster than revenues' | H1-25 | Net margin 20.5%, free-cash-flow margin 32.1%; earnings per share up 7.9% year over year on revenue up 6.1% | **Corroborated** -- the operating leverage is real, earnings per share outgrew revenue. |
| 'Expanding profitability / margin expansion' | Q3-25 | But the scan flags gross margin 27.8% (contracting); margin 0.57 standard deviations below the historical peak | **Partial divergence.** Reported operating margin expands, yet gross margin is contracting and the company sits below its own peak margin -- investment (research and development around 20% of revenue, the cloud migration) is squeezing the gross line even as operating leverage flatters operating profit. |
| 'Strong commercial momentum... acceleration' | Q3-25 | Revenue growth slowed to +6.1% year over year (2025) from +13% (2024); forward earnings-per-share growth +11.4% | **Story richer than the print.** 'Acceleration' is constant-currency and segment-cherry-picked; the reported top line decelerated hard from 2024's pace. |
| Buyback to 'repurchase and redeem all... shares' from the 2020 raise | FY24/Q1-25 | Net debt 2.68B (live, 2026-03-31), leverage about 1.0x; new EUR500M program March-June 2026 | **Corroborated but modest.** Real, disciplined, dilution-offsetting -- not a balance-sheet-transforming return. |
| 'Beat 6 of 7 quarters (86%)' | -- | Screener earnings-beat rate 86%; earnings-per-share 3-year annual growth +27.6% | **Corroborated.** A genuine guidance-delivery track record. |

**Net read:** the financials support the durability claim (recurring, high free cash flow, an 86% beat rate, earnings-per-share annual growth of +27.6%) but do not support the 'acceleration' framing -- 2025 was a deceleration year (revenue +6.1% versus the +13.3% three-year rate) dressed in constant-currency language. The story is modestly richer than the reported trajectory, mostly through the constant-currency and currency-adjusted lens and through segment selection.

---

## 3. Tone and confidence trajectory

**Trajectory: stable-to-mildly-declining offense, with a deliberate third-quarter re-offensive pivot to AI.**

- FY24 (rising, peak): 'highly productive year... delivered on our outlook and commitments.' Unhedged.
- Q1-25 (first hedge): 'Despite the broad uncertainty... we are focusing our '25 outlook excluding ForEx effects.' Volumes 'a bit below... but nevertheless quite good.'
- H1-25 (most defensive): 'challenging macro and geopolitical environment'; repeated 'hopefully we have seen the bottom'; free cash flow deterioration 'expected' in the first half, recovery promised in the second. This is the low point of confidence -- management is managing expectations down on volumes and up on second-half hope.
- Q3-25 (re-offensive): 'strong... full of momentum... revenue growth acceleration.' The defensiveness recedes; the company re-frames around AI leadership ('almost 20 years,' '500 use cases,' 'uniquely placed to infuse Agentic AI').

The shift from offensive (the NDC/Nevio land-grab) to defensive (foreign-exchange, macro, cloud-cost) language in mid-2025, then a re-offensive AI re-frame in the third quarter, is the key tonal signature. Guidance specificity decreased (moving to constant-currency and currency-adjusted framing, with a simplified presentation), a mild credibility cost.

---

## 4. Guidance credibility

**Reliability: high.** The screener corroborates an 86% earnings-beat rate (6 of 7) and an earnings-per-share three-year annual growth of +27.6% against a guided trajectory management consistently described as 'delivered on our outlook.' When the first half of 2025 disappointed on volumes and free cash flow, management did not cut full-year guidance -- they reaffirmed the currency-adjusted outlook and pointed to a second-half recovery, and the third-quarter print ('revenue growth acceleration') and the maintained FY2026 guidance (live: first-quarter 2026 up 7.9% in constant currency, within ranges) suggest the second-half promise broadly held. The one credibility wrinkle is the mid-year re-basing to constant currency, which makes the 'delivered' claim less testable. There is no evidence of systematic sandbagging -- beats are modest (latest-quarter earnings per share up 5% year over year), consistent with honest, slightly conservative guidance rather than lowballing.

---

## 5. Analyst pressure points

- **Nevio/Stratos conversion pace (recurring, escalating).** Sven Merkt (H1-25): 'what the main obstacles are to converting these deals.' James Goodall (Q3-25, sharper): 'we haven't seen a new customer for a while and Nevio still waiting for one on Stratos. Are you comfortable with the current pace?' Management's answer -- 'engagement is high... nothing is done until it's really done' -- is a classic non-answer deflection. This was the single most-pressed worry, and it later resolved favorably (live 2026: TUI signed as the Navitaire Stratos launch customer; Lufthansa Group adopted Nevio).
- **Free-cash-flow deterioration (Q1/H1-25).** Toby Ogg pushed twice on free cash flow down 22% and the building blocks for a second-half re-acceleration. Maroto's answer leaned on 'seasonality... working capital... taxes' -- vague, but the second-half recovery did materialize.
- **Booking-fee pricing durability.** Laurent Daure repeatedly probed whether the 5% lift in revenue per booking is structural or a one-off from full-content-to-free-negotiation contract resets. Management conceded the contract-reset tailwind 'probably will not be there' -- an honest admission that a pricing lever is maturing.
- **Hospitality guidance (payments and the Marriott ramp).** Analysts repeatedly questioned the second-half acceleration needed to hit the low end; management blamed media and US sentiment and a payments-implementation delay -- a recurring soft spot.

---

## 6. Red flags and green flags

Red flag: **gross margin contracting** (27.8%, below the historical peak) while research and development runs around 20% of revenue and cloud-migration costs persist -- heavy reinvestment is masking gross-line pressure.
Red flag: **constant-currency re-framing** in mid-2025 reduced guidance testability; 'acceleration' claims rest on constant currency plus segment selection while reported growth decelerated.
Red flag: **pricing-lever maturation** -- management openly conceded the contract-reset booking-fee tailwind fades.
Red flag: **AI/agentic look-to-book risk** -- global-distribution-system chief executives (Amadeus included) have publicly said AI is worsening look-to-book ratios (searches per booking); the AI-as-disintermediation tail is now an acknowledged industry debate, not a fringe fear.
Green flag: **86% beat rate, earnings-per-share annual growth +27.6%, free-cash-flow annual growth +33.8%** -- genuine, cash-backed delivery.
Green flag: **switching-cost moat intact** -- airline IT (Altea/Nevio) and hospitality central-reservation systems are multi-year, mission-critical, sticky migrations (Marriott, Accor, MGM, Ascott live or ramping).
Green flag: **Nevio/Stratos worry resolved** -- the most-pressed analyst concern (conversion pace) reversed favorably in 2026 (TUI on Stratos, Lufthansa on Nevio).
Green flag: **deleveraged** -- net-debt-to-EBITDA about 1.0x, free-cash-flow margin 32%, Piotroski 7 of 9, Altman-Z 3.0 (solvent).

---

## 7. Hidden signals

- **Terminology migrating from 'bookings' to 'AI / data mesh.'** Across the four calls the source of pride shifts from volume (bookings, passengers boarded) to technology leverage (cloud percentage, AI use-cases, data mesh). This is partly genuine strategy and partly a defensive re-narration away from a maturing, cyclical volume story toward an open-ended AI addressable market ('we have not revisited the EUR41B addressable market... in theory it should extend').
- **Capital-allocation language hardening.** Borg (Q3-25) gives the disciplined-capital-allocation rundown -- organic investment first, dividend, then 'M&A still remains... a key relevant part of our growth strategy' and 'we'll come back in February' with any change. This signals that the buyback is a bridge (cleaning up dilution), not a new permanent return policy, and that bolt-on acquisitions remain the preferred use of cash -- a mild negative for anyone hoping for faster shareholder returns.
- **New chief-financial-officer voice.** Borg is more precise on cost and cloud-lapping mechanics ('lapping of Vision-Box consolidation,' 'slowdown in underlying cost growth in FY26') -- a more controlled, finance-led narration than Maroto's commercial-optimism register. The change reads as professionalization, not a red flag.
- **Disintermediation candor.** In the third quarter, management volunteered that historical direct-sell disintermediation 'is clearly a reality' but 'we are seeing less in '25' -- an honest framing that the structural headwind exists but is being offset by share gains and content onboarding.

---

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

**Classification: stable** (a high-quality, cash-compounding infrastructure franchise in a deceleration-then-stabilization phase, re-narrating around AI). Not strengthening (growth decelerated from 2024's pace; gross margin contracting; pricing lever maturing), not deteriorating (delivery track record intact, moat sticky, deleveraged, the key analyst worry resolved favorably in 2026).

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** this is a quality compounder at a discount, not a special situation. The 'catalyst' (the EUR500M buyback running March to June 2026) is small (about 1.6% of market value, roughly 3.5% of shares), recurring, and dilution-offsetting -- already substantially executed and spent on the re-rating. The real question is whether the AI and agentic-distribution debate (look-to-book degradation; direct-retailing and tech-native disintermediation) is a fear-driven mispricing on an entrenched switching-cost backbone, or the early innings of a terminal erosion of the global-distribution-system toll-booth. The financials say the moat is paying (32% free-cash-flow margin, return on invested capital about 9.9%, sticky multi-year IT and central-reservation-system contracts), but the gross-margin contraction and the look-to-book candor say the franchise is investing hard to stay ahead of that threat -- so the value case rests on the threat being manageable (re-platforming captures the shift) rather than terminal (the backbone gets disintermediated).

**Moat:** type = switching costs plus network and scale (airline-IT migrations are multi-year and mission-critical; the travel platform is a two-sided air-content and travel-seller network; the global distribution system has scale economics). Width: wide but with a contracting gross margin -- return on invested capital about 9.9% is solid but not spectacular and roughly stable, cash conversion is elite, but the gross line is eroding under reinvestment. I read the durable earnings base as wide; the trend is the watch-item.
**Moat trend:** stable -- returns and cash conversion are holding; gross margin is contracting on reinvestment (cloud plus research and development at 20% of sales), but that reinvestment is defending and extending the moat (Nevio/Stratos/AI), not a sign of collapse. Not yet eroding at the franchise level.
**Secular threat:** manageable -- AI and agentic distribution and direct-airline retailing are real structural forces, but Amadeus is positioned as the re-platformer of the shift (NDC aggregation, Nevio offer and order, agentic AI on its own data mesh) with deep switching costs; this is a fear-print on an entrenched backbone, not a melting base, for now. It crosses to material if look-to-book economics break global-distribution-system unit pricing or a tech-native captures airline retailing at scale.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory stable; wide moat; moat trend stable; secular threat manageable.
